file_name: HEMISPHERX - Sales, Marketing, Distribution, and Supply Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: "Affiliate" means any corporation or other business entity, which controls, is controlled by, or is under the common control of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: "Affiliate" means any corporation or other business entity, which is related in any way to a Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Affiliate' aligns with standard legal definitions of control. The modified version replaces 'controls, is controlled by, or is under the common control of a Party' with 'is related in any way to a Party,' which broadens the definition to include entities with any kind of relationship, regardless of control. This contradicts the established legal meaning of 'affiliate,' especially in the context of securities laws where control is a key factor.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of 'affiliate' is overly broad and does not adhere to the concept of 'control' as defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Rule 12b-2. Rule 12b-2 specifies that an affiliate is someone that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified. The modification removes the concept of 'control' creating a conflict with the established legal standard.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'Affiliate' replaces the term 'controls, is controlled by, or is under the common control of a Party' with 'is related in any way to a Party,' broadening the definition. The Law Explanation specifies that Rule 12b-2 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 defines an affiliate based on the concept of 'control'. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, reinforces this by outlining definitions related to accelerated filers, which rely on affiliate status to determine market value. By removing the concept of control, the modified definition contradicts the established legal meaning of affiliate within securities law. The original definition aligns with the concept of control required by 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 while the modified definition does not.

perturbation.original_text: "HEMISPHERX Intellectual Property" means all HEMISPHERX patents, patent applications, know-how, and trademarks owned or controlled by HEMISPHERX up to the termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: "HEMISPHERX Intellectual Property" means all HEMISPHERX patents, patent applications, future inventions, and trademarks used by HEMISPHERX at any time.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'owned or controlled by HEMISPHERX up to the termination or expiration of this Agreement.' The modified text expands this to include 'future inventions' and removes the limitation regarding ownership or control only up to the agreement's termination, specifying 'used by HEMISPHERX at any time.' This creates a conflict because it implies that any invention HEMISPHERX uses, even if not legally protected or owned, is considered its intellectual property, contradicting standard IP law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition includes 'future inventions' and removes any restriction to inventions that are 'owned or controlled.' This contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 102 which defines the conditions for patentability, including novelty. An invention must be new to be patentable, meaning simply using an invention, especially one not yet patented or even conceived, does not automatically make it the intellectual property of the user, especially if it lacks novelty, thus creating a misaligned definition of Intellectual Property.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Novelty; Prior Art.— A person shall be entitled to a patent unless— (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued under section 151, or in an application for patent published or deemed published under section 122(b), in which the patent or application, as the case may be, names another inventor and was effectively filed before the effective filing date of the claimed invention. (b) Exceptions.— (1) Disclosures made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.— A disclosure made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of a claimed invention shall not be prior art to the claimed invention under subsection (a)(1) if— (A) the disclosure was made by the inventor or joint inventor or by another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; or (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such disclosure, been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor or another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor . (2) Disclosures appearing in applications and patents.— A disclosure shall not be prior art to a claimed invention under subsection (a)(2) if— (A) the subject matter disclosed was obtained directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such subject matter was effectively filed under subsection (a)(2), been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'HEMISPHERX Intellectual Property' to include 'future inventions' and removes the restriction that it must be 'owned or controlled by HEMISPHERX.' This contradicts the requirement for novelty in patent law as stated in 35 U.S.C. § 102, because it implies that any invention used by HEMISPHERX is considered its intellectual property, regardless of whether it is new or patentable. The scraped snippet from 35 U.S. Code § 102 states that 'A person shall be entitled to a patent unless—(1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention'. The modified definition attempts to assign intellectual property rights to items that might not meet this criteria, therefore creating a contradiction. If HEMISPHERX is using something that is already available to the public, this contradicts the idea of it being HEMISPHERX Intellectual Property, as per the original text.

perturbation.original_text: HEMISPHERX will have the option at any time to buy out this Agreement. If exercised within the first two (2) years HEMISPHERX will pay SCIEN three (3) times the Product sales for the preceding 12 months. If exercised after year 3, HEMISPHERX will pay SCIEN two (2) times the Product sales for the preceding 12 months.
perturbation.changed_text: HEMISPHERX will have the option at any time to buy out this Agreement. HEMISPHERX will pay SCIEN fair market value for the agreement. If there are disputes as to the fair market value, the determination will be made by HEMISPHERX alone.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines a specific formula for the buyout price. The modified text replaces this with 'fair market value' but then stipulates that HEMISPHERX alone determines this value. This creates a contradiction because 'fair market value' typically implies an objective, mutually agreed-upon price, or one determined by an independent appraiser. Granting HEMISPHERX sole discretion contradicts this concept, raising concerns about enforceability and good faith dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305. Open Price Term.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating HEMISPHERX alone determines 'fair market value', the modified text potentially violates the principles of good faith and commercial reasonableness implied in UCC § 2-305. This section addresses open price terms in contracts and implies that the price should be determined in good faith. Allowing one party to unilaterally determine fair market value without any objective standard or mechanism for dispute resolution contradicts this principle and could render the buyout clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing HEMISPHERX alone to determine 'fair market value,' contradicts the principle of good faith in determining price, as implied in UCC § 2-305. Snippet 1, particularly section (2), states that 'A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith.' The changed text gives HEMISPHERX sole discretion without any mechanism for ensuring good faith or reasonableness, which deviates from the implied requirement of fair dealing in setting the price.
